title: Supreme Court Declares Right to Walk on Safe Footpaths a Fundamental Right
summary: The Supreme Court of India has ruled that the right to walk on safe footpaths is a fundamental right, expanding the ambit of Article 21 (Right to Life). The Court directed authorities to ensure footpaths are constructed on all roads, making pedestrian infrastructure a constitutional obligation. The bench sought enactment of a law to give statutory backing to this right, underscoring the state's duty to protect pedestrian safety.
exam_angle: This ruling expands the interpretation of Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty) — a recurring UPSC theme. It connects to judicial activism, fundamental rights, and the concept of 'right to life' including the right to live with dignity and safety.
key_facts: Constitutional basis: Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty)
Court: Supreme Court of India
Directive: Footpaths to be provided on all roads
Court sought enactment of a dedicated law on pedestrian safety
source: The Indian Express / India Today / Business Standard

title: India-UK Free Trade Agreement: Absence of Investment Chapter Not a Concern, Says UK Negotiator
summary: Senior UK negotiator Muscatelli stated that the absence of an investment chapter in the India-UK Free Trade Agreement (FTA) is not a major concern, signalling flexibility in the deal's structure. The India-UK FTA has been under negotiation since January 2022 and covers goods, services, and intellectual property. The remark suggests both sides may finalise a goods-and-services-focused deal first, with investment provisions potentially addressed separately.
exam_angle: The India-UK FTA is a major bilateral trade development relevant to GS Paper 2 (IR) and GS Paper 3 (Economy). Questions can be framed around India's FTA strategy, trade negotiations, and bilateral economic relations.
key_facts: India-UK FTA negotiations launched: January 2022
Current status: Concluded/near-finalized deal without investment chapter
Negotiator statement by: Muscatelli (UK side)
Deal covers: Goods, services, intellectual property

title: India-US Trade Deal: Piyush Goyal Says Pact Cannot Be Implemented Without 'Competitive Advantage' for India
summary: Union Commerce Minister Piyush Goyal stated that the India-US trade deal cannot be finalised until India secures a 'competitive advantage', indicating that negotiations are still ongoing with key sticking points. The US, meanwhile, praised India's GST framework, calling it a positive factor that could boost bilateral trade ties. The remarks come amid broader India-US bilateral trade discussions targeting a deal before the 90-day tariff pause deadline.
exam_angle: India-US trade negotiations are critical for GS Paper 2 (IR) and GS Paper 3 (Economy). The US tariff situation, India's negotiating stance, and the role of GST in trade facilitation are all exam-relevant concepts.
key_facts: Minister involved: Piyush Goyal, Union Commerce Minister
US praised India's GST as trade-boosting
India's condition: Competitive advantage must be secured
Context: 90-day US tariff pause creating urgency for bilateral deal
source: The Times of India / The Economic Times / NDTV

title: Iran Negotiators Travel to Switzerland for Nuclear Peace Talks with US
summary: Iranian negotiators have departed for Switzerland to hold talks with the United States aimed at reaching a peace/nuclear deal. This represents a continuation of indirect diplomatic negotiations between the two countries over Iran's nuclear programme. Switzerland has been serving as a neutral venue and intermediary for US-Iran communications amid the absence of direct diplomatic relations.
exam_angle: Iran-US nuclear diplomacy is important for GS Paper 2 (IR), linking to nuclear non-proliferation, IAEA, and geopolitical dynamics in West Asia — a region frequently examined in UPSC Mains.
key_facts: Venue: Switzerland (neutral intermediary)
Parties: Iran and United States
Purpose: Nuclear peace deal discussions
Context: No direct US-Iran diplomatic relations; Switzerland acts as go-between

title: Modi Government Launches Initiative to Reverse India's Brain Drain
summary: The Modi government has announced an initiative aimed at bringing back Indian talent from abroad, addressing the long-standing issue of brain drain. The policy focuses on creating attractive opportunities for Indian scientists, researchers, and professionals to return and contribute to India's growing innovation and startup ecosystem. This is aligned with broader goals of Atmanirbhar Bharat and building domestic R&D and technology capabilities.
exam_angle: Brain drain reversal policy connects to GS Paper 2 (Governance) and GS Paper 3 (Economy/Sci-Tech). It is relevant to topics like human capital, India's demographic dividend, innovation ecosystem, and Atmanirbhar Bharat.
key_facts: Initiative by: Government of India (Modi government)
Objective: Reverse brain drain; attract Indian diaspora talent back
Context: Aligns with Atmanirbhar Bharat and innovation ecosystem goals
Focus sectors: Science, research, technology, startups

title: Nobel Laureate John Jumper Quits Google DeepMind to Join Anthropic
summary: John Jumper, who won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2024 for his work on AlphaFold protein structure prediction, has resigned from Google DeepMind and joined AI safety company Anthropic. Jumper's AlphaFold model revolutionised structural biology by accurately predicting protein structures. Anthropic, founded by former OpenAI researchers, focuses on AI safety research and is backed by Amazon and Google.
exam_angle: John Jumper is a prominent 'Person in News' for UPSC, connecting to the Nobel Prize 2024 (Chemistry), AlphaFold, and the broader AI industry landscape. Relevant for GS Paper 3 (Sci-Tech) and Prelims current affairs.
key_facts: John Jumper: Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2024 (AlphaFold protein prediction)
Previous organisation: Google DeepMind
Moved to: Anthropic (AI safety company)
AlphaFold: AI model that predicts 3D protein structures

title: India Calls for Greater Self-Reliance in Capital, Defence, Energy and Technology: Kotak
summary: Uday Kotak, founder of Kotak Mahindra Bank, called for India to reduce dependence on foreign capital, defence equipment, energy, and technology, emphasising the need for greater self-reliance. The statement aligns with the government's Atmanirbhar Bharat initiative and comes against the backdrop of global geopolitical uncertainty, US tariff pressures, and India's push for domestic manufacturing. The remarks highlight strategic vulnerabilities in India's current dependence on foreign sources in critical sectors.
exam_angle: Self-reliance in defence, energy, and technology is a core theme of GS Paper 3 (Economy/Defence/Sci-Tech) and connects to Atmanirbhar Bharat, strategic autonomy, and India's import substitution goals.
key_facts: Statement by: Uday Kotak, Founder, Kotak Mahindra Bank
Areas of concern: Foreign capital, defence, energy, technology dependence
Policy alignment: Atmanirbhar Bharat initiative
Context: Global geopolitical uncertainty and US tariff pressures

title: Karnataka Appoints Kunal Vajani and Omar Hoda as Additional Advocate Generals in Supreme Court
summary: The Karnataka government has appointed Kunal Vajani and Omar Hoda as Additional Advocate Generals to represent the state in the Supreme Court of India. Advocate Generals and Additional Advocate Generals are senior law officers appointed by state governments to handle their legal representation in courts. This appointment is significant for the state's ongoing litigation strategy at the apex court.
exam_angle: The position of Advocate General is a constitutional post under Article 165. This item is relevant for GS Paper 2 (Polity) — constitutional/legal officers of the state, and for persons in news.
key_facts: Appointees: Kunal Vajani and Omar Hoda
Role: Additional Advocate Generals for Karnataka in Supreme Court
Constitutional basis: Article 165 (Advocate General of State)
Appointed by: Karnataka State Government

title: India's IT Industry Eyes 'Unglamorous' AI Work Outsourcing from American Companies
summary: India's information technology industry is positioning itself to capture AI-related outsourcing work from American companies, particularly the 'unglamorous' but high-volume tasks such as data labelling, AI model training, testing, and fine-tuning. This represents a strategic pivot by Indian IT firms to remain relevant in the AI era, building on their existing outsourcing strengths. Industry leaders believe this can generate significant revenue and employment as global AI adoption accelerates.
exam_angle: India's IT sector pivot to AI services is relevant for GS Paper 3 (Economy/Sci-Tech), touching on India's services export strength, digital economy, employment, and competitiveness in the global AI value chain.
key_facts: Sector: Indian IT/BPO industry
Opportunity: AI-related outsourcing (data labelling, model training, testing)
Client base: American technology companies
Strategic context: India leveraging existing outsourcing strengths for AI era

title: Trump Administration Dismisses Security Concerns About Anthropic, Signals No AI Export Curbs
summary: US President Donald Trump's administration dismissed suggestions that Anthropic, the AI safety company, poses a national security threat, signalling that it would not impose AI-related export curbs on the company. The statement comes amid broader US-China tech rivalry and debates over AI regulation and export controls. Anthropic is backed by Google and Amazon and is considered one of the leading frontier AI labs globally.
exam_angle: US AI policy, export controls on technology, and the global AI governance debate are relevant for GS Paper 3 (Sci-Tech) and GS Paper 2 (IR). The item connects to US-China tech decoupling and AI regulation.
key_facts: Company: Anthropic (AI safety/frontier AI lab)
US position: Not a national security threat; no export curbs
Anthropic investors: Amazon and Google
Context: US-China tech rivalry and AI export control debates
